channel.title: Morgan Mayday
channel.copyright: Anticopyright. We all live in the public domain.

item.title: lakeside afternoon
item.description:
It’s sat on the edge of the little retaining wall, pants rolled up, feet dangling in the water, not making any effort to wash the wet sand from its soles. It stares out towards the other shore, fixated on seemingly nothing.

I settle in beside it, fold an arm around its waist, let its head fall onto my shoulder. I let it think. It needs time to work the words into a usable shape; it’ll be done when it’s done.

item.title: Why I'm going, mostly, sober
item.description:
So much antismoking propaganda focuses around one simple idea: “you’ll quit if you learn it’s bad for you.”

This is…well, in the modern day, who cares, right? We’re all gonna die sometime, might as well have smoko on the way out. Everyone I’ve talked to about smoking knows it’ll kill us if we don’t quit. The propaganda just feels like it’s babying us. (of course, I don’t think the demographic they’re targeting is the working poor, just highschoolers, but it’s still insulting.)

item.title: What can you do?
item.description:
They’re going to kill us all if we don’t do something.

We’ve all seen it. Even if we’ve tried to ignore it, it’s impossible to fully put from your mind. The State Wants To Kill Us. Immigrants in concentration camps, trans women being put on publicly-searchable registries and healthcare being outlawed, the continued panoptic militarisation of the police state powered by AI, record-breaking summers every summer even as data centers are built and built and built. The dying future is here, and the sentiment I hear from almost every voice is “yeah, the world’s ending, but I have work tomorrow.”

item.title: headed home
item.description:
My sister pushes its sunglasses up its nose, blows the cigarette smoke out the window. It streams out behind us, a carcinogenic ribbon lit by the setting sun, there and gone again.

“caroline,” she says, after a moment, “is this all there is?”

I glance at it, backlit by the sunset through the trees. The light catches the frizz of her hair, reveals the hidden hue under the black, distorts a little at the near-real line of the edge of her halo.

item.title: Meaning Your Apologies
item.description:
“Sorry” is all well and good, but what are you going to do about it?

I have a very strict heuristic set for apologies.
   — I only apologise for things I can do better on in future, or if they’re things I won’t have the chance to.
   — I only apologise for things I want to do better on in future, or if I regret them.
   — I must actually follow through on my apologies.
To violate any of these is to lie, and to paraphrase Margaret Killjoy, it’s only ethical to lie to people it’s ethical to punch.

item.title: on personhood
item.description: Personhood is a vector of violence used by Authority, a perpetual threat used to separate Desirables and Undesirables. When any Person considers a group or being Undesirable, they often decry that group or being as not a person, as subhuman, as no better than animals, as deserving nothing more, as being okay to strip of any right or freedom. You can see this happening—well, anywhere you look, really, but most definitely in USAmerican politics as we speak. Let’s draw a parallel.

item.title: site retune --- 01/07/2026
item.description:
“holy fuck you CAN do emdashes in markdown”
— me, thirty minutes ago, about to enter a perfectionist episode

anyways. i’ve been thinking about like. ideological coherence, and linguistic coherence, and how my website does not further either of those goals. my old glossary page (yes, it’s old now, i’m getting rid of it,) was…would have been so much better served by me just being clear and letting context do the rest.

item.title: The Myth of (Healthy) Self Control
item.description: If you don’t know me, the statement “I have near-zero self control” would sound, uh, unhealthy? Concerning, if you know I own a gun, for sure. Maybe a lie, if you know me a little bit. If you know me better, you’d probably go “oh, is this one of those semantic hills you’re dying on again?” to which I say “i dont know what you’re fucking talking about, i’m not gonna die on this hill any time soon. pass me that grenade there’s another cluster coming up this side.” It’s true, though—I’ve cultivated a me with near-zero self control, because I’ve cultivated a me that doesn’t need it.

item.title: sharp edges
item.description:
Estimates put the number of civilian-owned firearms in the US at 500 million. The US population is, at time of writing, estimated at around 342 million bodies.

i press on the disassembly plate, pull the slide off, set it down, and hand you the receiver. see, she can’t shoot, taken apart like this. feel the contour of her grip, the featherlight weight without the slide. there’s no need to worry, honey, you can’t hurt me with her like this. hold her while we talk, yeah? holding a stripped-down receiver, there’s this … vulnerability, almost, to the gun. inert, unable to fire, like a knife without a blade, a hand without movement, a voice without words. this thing that is made for the express purpose of violence and death, and she’s naked and open under your hands, rendered near—but never wholly—harmless, while you care for her. can you feel it?

item.title: killjoy
item.description:
people in real life: hey man hows it going

various (meme)

There is a tendency to assign neutrality to the masculine form of words in English, mostly in slang—“hey, man,” “hey, dude,” “king shit”—though it shows up sometimes in general phrasing still, feminist pushes mostly have made things like “fireman” over “firefighter” obsolete. This is, overall, something most people do, and something most people find pretty harmless. A lot of people will go through their lives not thinking about it, or thinking about it a tiny bit at most.

item.title: 404 Page Not Found
item.description:
That page couldn’t be found.

Back to homepage.
